The Script Structure  
Our player is functional in that he is able to run around, but it would be nice to add some  
attack functionality to him. We'll accomplish that through a little bit of script logic, but,  
ﬁrst, it's time to explain how scripts really work.  
Start by bringing up the Player Actor's script for edit.  
The script only has one line in it, but it's placed in the section entitled STARTUP, which  
means it will only run when the actor owning this script activates. The SHUTDOWN  
section is the parallel to that - it runs only when the actor shuts down. We'll talk more  
about how actors can start up and shut down, but, for now, it is reasonable to say the  
player starts up when the game starts and then never shuts down.  
Encapsulating the startup and shutdown sections is the PRESCRIPT, which is the  
section that runs all the time while the actor is active. Anything placed in here will run  
constantly, meaning, more speciﬁcally, every frame. Our game's scripts run every single  
frame at 60 frames per second, from the top until it's told to stop or it hits the end of the  
script.  
2 of 16  
01/11/2016 03:24 PM  
Ch. 3: Basic Scripting - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._3:_Basic_Scripting  
Actors, when active, are running their scripts all the time, so an important part of your  
script structure is controlling what should and shouldn't be happening. We have several  
methods at your disposal, but the simplest one, which we will employ for our player, is  
the behavior. Actors are allowed to run a single behavior each frame, so they're a very  
straightforward way of sectioning oﬀ bits of script from one another.  
Adding a Behavior  
Add a new behavior by right clicking below the prescript area and selecting new  
behavior at the top of the menu.  
A new behavior will be added called New Behavior by default, go ahead and name it  
idle.  
Now, add another behavior below idle and call it attacking.  
3 of 16  
01/11/2016 03:24 PM  
Ch. 3: Basic Scripting - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._3:_Basic_Scripting  
Only one behavior will execute per frame, and, by default, the topmost behavior will run.  
The script, as it currently stands, will immediately be in the idle behavior and will never  
change to attacking. Let's set up a simple attack for the player.  
Script Interface Tips  
Placement of New Commands  
We're about to add a new script command, but it's worth mentioning the logic for where  
new commands are inserted based upon your mouse click. A new line will always appear  
above the place you are right-clicking, so if you want to add a line somewhere, just  
right-click on the line below it. This is to prevent you from having to click between the  
lines, which is not an obvious location in the UI. Just remember the new line is always  
going above where you click, and you'll get the hang of it. [diagram]  
Navigating the Menus  
Additionally, though you can navigate the pulldowns by mousing over an item with a  
submenu, waiting for it to appear, then moving your mouse horizontally, it's even faster if  
you just click the submenu - your mouse will immediately warp sideways into the  
submenu's contents, and picking items from submenus becomes more of an exercise in  
vertical mouse movement as opposed to having to mouse sideways. [diagram]  
Logic for Parameters of New Commands  
Finally, you may notice that when you add a new command, it defaults it to something  
that may or may not make sense. The system tries to pick the last thing you talked about  
and make the newly-created command refer to that. It's not necessarily what you want,  
but it explains what you're seeing when you add new commands, especially if you're  
confused that your new lines don't necessarily look like the new lines in the pictures here.  
Adding A Player Attack Check  
Now we can really start adding some scripting logic! What we'll do in this segment is  
check if the player has pressed the Action 1 button (A on Wii, A on 360, and X on PS3),  
then halt his movement while he plays an attack animation.  
Check Value  
4 of 16  
01/11/2016 03:24 PM  
Ch. 3: Basic Scripting - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._3:_Basic_Scripting  
Would you believe it if you heard you're about to learn the most powerful command in our  
scripting language as the ﬁrst command? You better believe it, as the simplest and most  
powerful logical check in our language is the check value command, which compares  
one number against another. We're going to use it to check the value of our controller's A  
button.  
Add a new check value command in the idle behavior.  
Change the lefthand side to check my > *controller's > action 1's > pressure  
state.  
Click to Expand  
Change the righthand side to check if it is 3: just pressed.  
The completed line, which, in plain English, is asking if our *controller's action 1  
button was just pressed, should look like this:  
The power of check value is not that checking values is inherently cool, but more that  
we have many diﬀerent values available to check - like the state of a controller's button -  
which provide meaningful information.  
5 of 16  
01/11/2016 03:24 PM  
Ch. 3: Basic Scripting - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._3:_Basic_Scripting  
Indentations  
Unlike some of the commands we've used in script, previously, you'll note that the check  
value creates an indentation. The indentation means, in all cases, if the command  
succeeds, and can be compared with an if in a general programming sense.. In the case  
of a check value, it means if the check succeeds. In this case, more explicitly, we are  
asking: does the lefthand side equal the righthand side?  
What this means is that we've written our very ﬁrst piece of decision-making in script -  
we can check if something is true, and then decide to act diﬀerently depending on the  
result.  
Set Behavior  
Add a new command, set behavior, inside the indentation of check value.  
If it hasn't already been set by default, change the parameter to be the attacking  
behavior.  
When you're done, your script should look like this.  
At this point, if we were to run, our bit of script logic would say: if the controller's action  
1 was pressed, change to the attacking behavior. There are two issues, still: 1) pressing  
the button doesn't actually do anything, and 2) there's no way to return to the idle  
behavior.  
IMPORTANT: If you are running now, you may note that the player leaps whenever  
you press the Action 1 button. This is because of some old, aforementioned  
functionality where players pay attention to their init jump velocity. You can and  
should disable this now by setting it to 0 on the Player Actor in the Actor Panel.

Playing an Animation  
In this segment, we'll solve the aforementioned problems by giving the player an  
animation to play. We will then use the animation as a basis for when the attack is done,  
so we can return to the idle behavior when it completes.  
The Model Editor in Brief  
The ﬁrst step towards playing an animation is actually knowing what animation to play!  
This is where the TFB Model Editor comes into play. While your level is saved in a format  
for the Level Editor (you may have noticed the ﬁle saves as a .lvl), models are in a format  
for our Model Editor (.mdls). Models contain the mesh, animations, and attach nodes for  
a character.  
Bring up the model editor by clicking the Edit button next to the model in the Actor  
Panel.  
If asked if you want to take edit control, say Don't Edit.  
The model editor will appear, loading the Air Dragon character (which will start  
animating) and his animation palette, which will be listed in the righthand panel.  
7 of 16  
01/11/2016 03:24 PM  
Ch. 3: Basic Scripting - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._3:_Basic_Scripting  
You can click the various animations and press the play button to preview it, as well  
as move slider to scrub through the animation. You can rotate/adjust the view with  
the same controls as used in the Level Editor (Alt+Left-Click, etc.).  
Feel free to scroll through the animations, but the one we are going to use for the  
attack is in slot 79, called AirDragon_LevelUp.ma.  
NOTE: I'm selecting this one because it is a base type animation, meaning it  
will override the current animation, as opposed to some of the character's other  
attacks (which are partial animations and just add on). We'll talk more about  
animation types later.  
Playing the Animation  
Remember the slot number, 79, and head back over to the Level Editor, closing  
down the Model Editor or just clicking away.  
Just below the set behavior line, add the play command.  
Change the lefthand parameter to (ANIMATIONS) > [animation palette] >  
<self>.  
Adjust the value parameter next to the oﬀset by to 79. The ﬁnal line should look like  
8 of 16  
01/11/2016 03:24 PM  
Ch. 3: Basic Scripting - tfbwiki  
this.  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._3:_Basic_Scripting  
[animation palette] is a set (as denoted by the [] brackets) of all the animations on an  
actor - equivalent to what you saw in the Model Editor. By setting the oﬀset by, you're  
saying play the member at index 79 of my [animation palette] set (indices start at  
0), which corresponds to the animation we saw earlier.  
Tracking the Animation  
At this point, you could try building and launching. You should be able to press Action 1  
and watch your character play the animation. This will only work once, though - while he  
is, indeed, playing the animation, we still haven't created logic to get him to return to the  
idle behavior. To do this, we'll need a way of tracking the animation we just played, and,  
for that, we'll need to create a variable.  
Variable Types  
There are, essentially, three types of variables in our system:  
value variables, which are numbers, for example: a distance, an angle, or a color.  
*reference variables, denoted by the asterisk (*), which are pointers to things, for  
example: a controller, an animation, or a sound.  
[set] variables, denoted by the square braces ([]), which are lists of things, for  
example: the [animation palette] or a list of actors.  
We've been interacting with each type using variables built-in to objects (e.g. our Player  
Actor's *controller reference or [animation palette]). In this case, because we want to  
hang on to the animation we're playing, we will need to create our own animation  
*reference.  
Creating a Reference  
In the STARTUP of your script, create a new variable by opening the create  
variable submenu in the command list and selecting an (ANIMATION) *reference.  
9 of 16  
01/11/2016 03:24 PM  
Ch. 3: Basic Scripting - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._3:_Basic_Scripting  
It will start oﬀ named New Animation Ref, but let's rename it to attack animation  
(yes, spaces are ok).  
At this point, you have created a new variable which is typed as an animation reference.  
References are not generic and may only point at things of the type that matches them  
(the same goes for sets). This helps prevent errors but also provides the context for our  
menus - for example, we know to display parameters relating to an animation when you  
inspect the properties of the animation reference.  
Note: Create Variable Placement  
While variables are created using the create variable command, it is a special command  
in that it carries no run-time implications (and is really not much of a command at all).  
Where you actually place the command has no impact, and the variable is created when  
the script starts up, no matter what. As a good practice, it's prudent to either put all your  
created variables in the STARTUP to keep them grouped together, or place them near  
where they are used, in the case of a very long script that does many things.  
Setting the Reference  
Now that you have created a reference, we can use it to actually hang on to the animation  
that's being played.  
Inside the indentation of the play animation line, add a new set *reference  
command.  
10 of 16  
01/11/2016 03:24 PM  
Ch. 3: Basic Scripting - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._3:_Basic_Scripting  
Since you just created the *attack animation variable, the system will ﬁll in the  
parameters with it.  
Change the righthand side to assign *attack animation to ^playing's > <self>.  
What this line says, in plain English, is: set the *attack animation reference to point at  
the animation I just played. The reason you have access to the ^playing parameter is  
because you placed the reference inside the indentation of the play command. This is  
what we call a scoped variable - once the script ﬂows out of the indentation, you'll lose  
track of it. We couldn't get any information about the played animation before setting the  
reference, since we're all the way in the attacking behavior when we want to know  
about it. Now, with our reference variable, we can!  
Checking the Animation  
Now that we have a hook to the *attack animation, we can add some logic to the  
attacking behavior to change behaviors once the animation completes. We will  
accomplish this by checking the time remaining value of the *attack animation.  
Add a new check value command to the attacking behavior.  
Change the lefthand side to check the (LOCALS) > *attack animation's > time  
remaining.  
11 of 16  
01/11/2016 03:24 PM  
Ch. 3: Basic Scripting - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._3:_Basic_Scripting  
The line should now be complete. Add a new set behavior command to the check  
value's indentation, so we switch back to the idle behavior when the animation  
complete. The ﬁnal line should look as follows.  
We now have a complete loop! Build and launch the game, press Action 1, and watch the  
fun!  
Adding Animation Logic  
I'm sure, by now, you've noticed a ﬂaw - once the player plays the attack animation, he  
stops playing his idle animation and never resumes. Even before playing the animation,  
he never plays a walk or a run - he just slides around. Let's resolve that by giving him  
some logic in the idle behavior to determine if he should play the idle, walk, or run  
animation.  
Open the Air Dragon's model in the Model Editor (or refer back to it if you left it  
open). Note the top slots which contain an Idle, Walk, and Run animation (Slots 0, 1,  
and 3, respectively). Remember those slots, as we're going to use them.  
Add a new check value command to the beginning of the idle behavior (above the  
check value for the controller).  
Change the lefthand side to check my > velocity's > horizontal length.  
12 of 16  
01/11/2016 03:24 PM  
Ch. 3: Basic Scripting - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._3:_Basic_Scripting  
Change the = to read > (greater than). The line should read like this:  
Insert a play command to play the Run animation (slot 3) in the case where his  
velocity's horizontal length is greater than 0.  
Using an Else  
Now he will explicitly try to play the run when he is moving. We could have him play his  
idle by adding another check which checks if his velocity is 0, but it would be better to  
add an else case, which runs if the main block is not true (in this case, the check value).  
Left-click the continue on the check value and select else.  
Note that the check expands to contain a true section and an else section, which  
will execute if the check fails.  
13 of 16  
01/11/2016 03:24 PM  
Ch. 3: Basic Scripting - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._3:_Basic_Scripting  
Add another play command to play the Idle animation (slot 0) in the else section, so  
he plays his idle if his velocity is not over 0 (i.e. he's stationary).  
Using an Else-If  
If you save the player's script, you should see that he properly plays his run animation  
while moving and then returns to his idle when he stops. If you recall viewing the Air  
Dragon in the model editor, he had 3 animations - an idle, a walk, and a run. We can use  
an else-if statement create multiple cases for diﬀerent values of the player's velocity.  
Left-click the ﬂow continue in the true section of the check value, and select  
else-if.  
You'll now have an additional else-if case added to your check value. Note that,  
unlike the regular else, you are allowed to specify a varying value on the righthand  
side (though the lefthand side must remain the same).  
14 of 16  
01/11/2016 03:24 PM  
Ch. 3: Basic Scripting - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._3:_Basic_Scripting  
Checks with elses/else-if's are evaluated from top to bottom. If you left the else-if as is  
(with the check for velocity checking 0), the bottom else would never execute - logically,  
if the actor's velocity isn't greater than 0, it will be equal to 0, and thus ﬂow into the  
else-if before reaching the else. Since we know the check executes from top to bottom,  
we can create a sort of ﬁlter to catch things in the right order.  
Adjust the initial check value component to read > 8 (just an arbitrary speed where  
we want the run to start playing), then change the else-if to read > 0. Now all 3  
cases have an opportunity to execute.  
Finally, add a play command to the else-if section to play the Walk (slot 1)  
animation. Your ﬁnal script should look like this:  
In plain English, this says: if going faster than 8 feet/sec, play the run animation; if not  
going faster than 8 but going faster than 0, play the walk animation; if neither of those  
15 of 16  
01/11/2016 03:24 PM  
Ch. 3: Basic Scripting - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._3:_Basic_Scripting  
(i.e. not moving at all), play the idle animation.  
Once you've live-updated and checked out your results, continue on to Chapter 4:  
Intermediate Scripting.
